I stared at him. The fluorescent light buzzed. My pencil had rolled off the table and was lying on the floor between us, but neither of us moved to pick it up.

"How do you know that?"

"Because I've already transcribed them. All of them. I know every mark she made, every underlining, every question she wrote in the margins. She was the one who pushed him to revise the ending of Jude. Not his editors. Her."

I should have been grateful. He was giving me information—valuable, specific information that could save me weeks of work. But all I felt was the sting of being outpaced. Of being seen.

"You could have told me that without coming here," I said.

"I could have." He didn't move. "But I wanted to see your face when you realized I was right."

I stood up. The chair scraped against the floor. "Is that what this is about? Being right?"

"Partly." His eyes held mine. Gray-green, the color of tidal water. "And partly because I wanted to know why you're so determined to hate me."

The question landed like a stone in still water. I had no answer that wouldn't reveal too much.

"I don't hate you," I said. It came out quieter than I intended.

"You don't like me."

"I don't know you."

"You've decided not to."

I picked up my pencil from the floor, mostly to have something to do with my hands. "You took my parking spot."

"You were circling for eleven minutes. I counted."

I looked at him sharply. He was smiling—just a ghost of one, there and gone.

"That's not funny."

"It's a little funny."

"It's not." But my voice had lost its edge. I hated that. I hated that he could do that—find the crack in my armor and press, gently, until something gave.

He set the folder on the table. "I brought you copies of my transcriptions. They're organized by year. You'll find everything you need."

I looked at the folder. Then at him. "Why?"

"Because you're good at what you do. And because—" He paused. His hand rested on the folder, fingers spread. "I'd rather compete with you than against you."

I opened my mouth to say something sharp, something that would put the wall back up. But what came out was: "That's not what you said in the parking lot."

"In the parking lot, I didn't know you yet."

He left before I could answer. The door clicked shut behind him. The fluorescent light kept humming. And I stood there, holding my pencil, staring at a folder I hadn't earned, feeling something I couldn't name.

I told myself it was irritation. I told myself I would use his notes and never thank him.

But my hand was shaking when I opened the folder.

samples.title: Enemies-to-Lovers dialogue
samples.content:
I found him in the department lounge two days later, staring at a kettle that had clearly been empty for some time. The faculty meeting had just ended, and the room still smelled of stale coffee and academic grievances. I should have walked past. I had a stack of essays to grade and a theory to prove and absolutely no reason to speak to the man who had left his notes in my archive box like a dare. But my hand was shaking when I opened the folder, and I hadn't stopped thinking about why. "The kettle won't boil if you don't turn it on," I said. He didn't turn around. "I'm aware of how a kettle works, Dr. Ashford."

"Then why are you staring at it like it owes you money?"

A pause. He set the kettle down and finally faced me. Those gray-green eyes caught the fluorescent light and turned it into something almost warm. My chest tightened. "You read my notes."

"You left them in a box I was scheduled to review."

"You left yours in mine first."

His mouth curved, just slightly. "So we're even."

"We're not even close to even." I stepped into the room, letting the door swing shut behind me. "You took my parking spot. You undermined me in front of the department head. And now you're standing in the lounge, not making tea, critiquing my work to my face."

"Critiquing?" He tilted his head. "I said I was thinking about it. That's not a critique."

"What were you thinking?"

The question came out sharper than I intended, a blade disguised as curiosity. He held my gaze for a long moment, and I watched something shift behind his eyes—a calculation, maybe, or a decision. "I was thinking," he said slowly, "that you might be right."

Silence. The fluorescent hum filled the space between us. "About what?" My voice had gone quieter. I hated that. "About the Hardy revisions. The marginalia. The timing." He crossed his arms, but it wasn't defensive—it was deliberate, a way of anchoring himself. "I didn't want to admit it when I first read your notes. I came looking for a flaw. Something I could use."

"And?"

"And I didn't find one."

The admission landed like a stone in still water. I didn't know what to do with it. I had prepared for condescension, for dismissal, for the same cool superiority he'd worn in the parking lot. Not for this. Not for him looking at me like I was something he hadn't expected to find. "Why are you telling me this?" I asked. "Because I don't like owing people." He uncrossed his arms, let them fall to his sides. "And because I think you're the only person in this department who actually reads the primary sources."

It wasn't a compliment. It was too precise for that, too measured. But it landed somewhere under my ribs anyway. "I don't need your approval, Julian."

"I know." His voice dropped, just slightly. "That's what makes it worth giving."

I should have left then. I should have turned around and walked out and told myself that none of it meant anything. But I stayed, my hand gripping the strap of my bag, my heart beating a rhythm I refused to name. "Your notes were good," I said. The words came out before I could stop them. "The secondary sources you flagged—I hadn't considered the overlap."

He went still. "That's almost a compliment, Dr. Ashford."

"Don't get used to it."

"I wouldn't dream of it."

But he was smiling. Not the sharp, competitive smile I'd seen in the parking lot. Something smaller. Something that looked almost human. I left before I could see what it turned into. And I told myself, as I walked back to my office, that the warmth in my chest was just the stale coffee. That the way his voice had softened on my name meant nothing. That I would avoid him, outwork him, out-publish him, and never think about the way he'd looked at me when he said he couldn't find a flaw. I was wrong about that too.

samples.title: Enemies-to-Lovers cliffhanger
samples.content:
{
  "title": "Enemies-to-Lovers cliffhanger",
  "content": "I didn't sleep that night. 

I lay in the dark of my apartment, the ceiling fan clicking in its lazy rotation, and I replayed the moment in the lounge. The way he'd said my name—not as a weapon, not as a challenge, but as if he were testing how it felt in his mouth. The way his hand had paused on the kettle, the way he'd looked at me when he said he couldn't find a flaw. 

I told myself it meant nothing. That he was playing a game I didn't understand the rules of yet. That Julian Croft didn't do anything without calculation. 

But when I closed my eyes, I saw his face in the archive light—the slight furrow between his brows, the way he'd leaned in when I spoke, as if my words mattered. And I saw him in the parking lot, stepping out of that black sedan, looking at me like I was a problem he wanted to solve. 

The worst part was that I wanted to be solved. 

I got up at 4 AM and drafted an email. Professional. Cold. A thank-you for the notes, nothing more. I read it seven times, my finger hovering over send, and then I deleted it. 

Because I didn't want to thank him. I wanted to ask him why he'd looked at me like that. Why he'd said my name like he was learning a new language. Why he'd left before I could answer. 

I told myself I would see him at the faculty mixer on Friday. I told myself I would be professional, distant, unreadable. I told myself I had everything under control. 

But when I finally drifted into restless sleep, the last thing I saw was the way he'd looked at me in the lounge—and the last thing I felt was the heat of something I refused to name."

samples.title: The Weight of Stillness
samples.content:
I remember the way the dust motes hung in the afternoon light, suspended like tiny worlds in the beam that cut through the kitchen window. They moved only when my mother passed through them, her apron brushing the counter as she kneaded dough. The flour rose in clouds, settling on her forearms, on the worn wooden table, on my bare feet where I sat on the floor, watching.

She didn't speak. She never did during those hours, not the way other mothers might hum or sing or ask about school. Her silence was a room I learned to inhabit, its walls made of her concentration, its floor the rhythm of her hands pressing and folding. I was five, maybe six. Old enough to know that questions were like stones dropped into deep water — they sank and left no trace.

The dough was a living thing under her palms. She pressed, and it resisted. She folded, and it sighed. I wanted to touch it, to feel that strange elasticity, but I knew better. Her body was a fence around her work, and I was on the other side.

Outside, the cicadas had begun their late afternoon chorus, a sound so constant it was almost a silence itself. The heat pressed against the glass, making the world beyond the window waver. I could see the garden hose coiled like a green snake by the back steps, the rusted bicycle my brother had abandoned weeks ago, the line of laundry hanging limp and still.

Then she stopped. Her hands rested on the dough, and she looked at me. Not through me, not past me — at me. Her eyes were the color of wet earth, and they held something I couldn't name. Not love, not impatience. A kind of recognition, as if she had forgotten I was there and was surprised to find me still.

She lifted her right hand from the dough, flour dusting her knuckles, and reached toward me. Her fingers, warm and slightly sticky, touched my cheek. Just once. A single point of contact, like a period at the end of a sentence.

Then she turned back to her work, and the silence resumed.

I stayed on the floor until the light shifted, until the dust motes stopped their dance and the kitchen grew dim. I didn't know then what that moment meant — only that it had weight, that it pressed into me like a seed into soil. It would take years to understand that some love is given not in words but in gestures so small they seem accidental, and that the absence of noise can be the most honest language of all.

samples.title: The Broken Jar
samples.content:
My father's voice came from the doorway, low and measured, the way he always spoke when he was about to decide something that had already been decided. I was twelve, sitting on the kitchen floor with a jar of honey I'd dropped. The glass had shattered across the linoleum, golden threads pooling between the shards, and I was trying to pick them up with my bare fingers, the cuts already blooming along my thumb.

"Leave it," he said.

I didn't move. My mother stood at the counter, her back to us, her hands still in the dough. She didn't turn around.
The word stay rose in the throat and went nowhere.

"I said leave it."

My thumb was bleeding into the honey, turning it a thin pink. I could feel the sting, but what I felt more was the space between them — the way my mother's shoulders had gone still, the way my father's shadow filled the doorway. No one had yelled. That was the worst part. The silence was louder than any scream.

I looked up at him. He was wearing his work boots, the ones with the cracked leather, and he hadn't taken them off at the door like he was supposed to. A fleck of dried mud clung to the lace. I wanted to say something — to explain it was an accident, that I'd been reaching for the bread, that my hands were small and the jar was slick — but I could feel the shape of the words in my throat, and they wouldn't come out.

"She's bleeding," my mother said. Not to him. To the dough.

He stepped into the room. The floorboards groaned under his weight. He didn't look at her. He looked at me, and I saw something in his eyes I didn't have a name for yet — not anger, but something older, something tired. He crouched down, slowly, the way a man does when his knees have started to ache. He didn't touch me. He just looked at my hand, at the honey and blood mixed together, and then he reached into his pocket and pulled out a handkerchief I'd never seen before.

"Press this," he said. "Hard."

I took it. The fabric was rough, smelled of tobacco and metal. I pressed it against my thumb and watched the red bloom through the white.

He didn't tell me it would be okay. He didn't apologize for the sharpness in his voice. He just stood up, walked to the closet, and came back with a broom. He swept the glass into a dustpan, slowly, deliberately, the way he did everything. My mother still hadn't turned around. The dough had started to dry on her fingers.

When he was done, he put the broom back and stood in the doorway again. "Next time," he said, "wait for the broom."

That was all. He left. The screen door slapped shut behind him.

I sat on the floor with the handkerchief pressed to my hand, watching the honey dry into a sticky film on the linoleum. My mother finally turned. She looked at me, then at the spot where he'd been standing, then back at me. She didn't say anything. She just wet a rag at the sink, knelt down, and wiped the floor clean. The motion was slow, circular, almost ceremonial. When she was done, she took the rag to the sink and wrung it out, the water running pink.

I didn't know then that this was how we loved each other — not in the words we said, but in the things we cleaned up afterward. The broken glass. The blood. The silence that followed. I didn't know that some confrontations don't end with a resolution, but with a broom and a wet rag and the sound of someone leaving.

samples.title: The Spaces Between
samples.content:
The kitchen had gone quiet after the jar broke. My mother's hands were still dusted with flour, but she had stopped kneading. She stood at the counter, staring at nothing, her back to me. The light had shifted, the dust motes now drifting in a lower beam, closer to the floor.

I wanted to say something, but the words felt like stones in my mouth.

My father had left. The front door clicked shut, the car engine turned over, and then there was only the sound of tires on gravel, fading. I sat on the floor, the honey still sticky on my fingers, the glass shards swept into a neat pile near the trash. My mother hadn't asked me to help. She had just done it, moving around me like I was furniture, her face blank. They took half a step forward. Then stopped, as if the floor had changed its mind.

She turned now, finally, and looked at me. Her eyes were dry, but something in them was gone, folded inward. She opened her mouth, then closed it. Shook her head.

"Mom?"

She didn't answer. She walked to the sink and turned on the water, letting it run over her flour-caked hands. The water was too hot — steam rose, and she didn't flinch. I watched her scrub, the movements mechanical, the way you wash something you don't want to touch but have to.

"Are you okay?" I asked. The question felt stupid even as I said it.

She shut off the water. Dried her hands on a towel. Then she crossed the room and knelt in front of me, her knees cracking on the linoleum. She took my sticky hand in her clean one and held it. She didn't say anything. She just held my hand, her thumb tracing a slow circle on my palm.

I could hear the clock in the hallway, ticking. A bird outside. My own breathing.

"It's not your fault," she said finally. Her voice was flat, but not cold. Tired. "None of this is your fault."

She didn't say anything else. She stood up, went back to the counter, and started kneading again, the dough taking shape under her palms. I stayed on the floor, the warmth of her hand still on my skin, the silence pressing in around us like a living thing. I understood then that some questions don't have answers, and some love is spoken only in the spaces between words, in the weight of a hand held too long, in the refusal to let go.

samples.title: The Weight of Silence
samples.content:
The kitchen door swung shut behind him, and the sound of it was a door closing on something more than the room. My mother's hand was still on my shoulder, her fingers trembling slightly, or perhaps that was me. I couldn't tell anymore where her body ended and mine began.

The honey had been cleaned up, the jar swept into the dustbin, but the sweetness lingered in the air, cloying and wrong. I wanted to say something, to break the silence that had settled over us like a second skin. But the words stuck in my throat, thick as the honey that had pooled on the floor.

She turned away from me, her hand falling from my shoulder, and walked to the sink. She turned on the faucet, and the water ran, loud and useless, as she stared out the window at the darkening yard. The light was almost gone now, the dust motes no longer visible, just the faint outline of her in the fading day. They looked at the door as if it had already chosen for them.

I stayed on the floor, my legs numb, my heart a slow, heavy drum. I wanted her to turn around, to say something, to tell me that everything would be all right, that the broken jar was just a jar, that my father would come back, that we would be fine. But she didn't turn. She just stood there, her back to me, the water running, and I knew that this was the closest I would ever get to an answer.

Some questions don't have answers, and some love is spoken only in the spaces between words, in the weight of a hand held too long, in the refusal to let go. But I was twelve, and I didn't know that yet. All I knew was the silence, the smell of honey, and the way my mother's shoulders curved forward, as if she was holding something in that she could never let out.

The water stopped. She dried her hands on her apron, slow and deliberate, and then she turned. Her face was calm, empty, a door closed. She looked at me, and for a moment, I thought I saw something flicker in her eyes—a crack, a question, a goodbye. But then it was gone, and she said, "Come. Let's get you to bed."

I stood up, my legs unsteady, and followed her out of the kitchen. The house was dark, the hallways long and quiet. I didn't look back at the broken jar, at the spot where my father had stood, at the silence that had swallowed us whole. I just followed my mother, her hand finding mine in the dark, her fingers cold and dry, and I held on, because that was all I knew how to do.

That night, I lay in bed, the smell of honey still in my nose, the weight of her hand still on my shoulder. I didn't sleep. I listened to the house settle, to the creak of the floorboards, to the sound of my mother moving in the kitchen, alone. And I knew, in the way that children know things they can't explain, that nothing would ever be the same.

The broken jar had been swept away, but the silence remained. And it would stay, pressing into me like a seed into soil, growing in the dark, waiting for the day I would finally understand that some love is spoken only in the refusal to let go.
